summary:
The 2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami, which struck on December 26, 2004, was one of the deadliest natural disasters in recorded history. The earthquake, measuring magnitude 9.1–9.3, occurred off the northwest coast of Sumatra, Indonesia, and was the third-largest earthquake ever recorded. It released energy equivalent to 23,000 Hiroshima atomic bombs, rupturing approximately 1,600 kilometers of fault line and triggering a massive tsunami that radiated outward across the Indian Ocean.

The tsunami struck coastlines across fourteen countries, with catastrophic impact in Indonesia's Aceh province, Thailand, Sri Lanka, and India. Waves reaching up to 30 meters devastated coastal communities throughout the region. The death toll exceeded 227,000 people, making it the deadliest tsunami in recorded history. A massive international humanitarian response delivered billions of dollars in aid, and the disaster dramatically accelerated the development of Indian Ocean tsunami warning systems that had not existed. The disaster drew global attention to the vulnerability of densely populated tropical coastlines and prompted reassessment of disaster preparedness in the affected regions.

While technically occurring in 2004, the earthquake and tsunami, and particularly the international response it generated, stand as a significant event in the closing phase of the twentieth-century humanitarian and disaster response framework.
